news_summary: Advancements in justice and technology are empowering human rights through Hungary's engagement with the ICC and an AI chatbot aiding migrants in Italy.
news_summary_fr: Les progrès réalisés dans les domaines de la justice et de la technologie contribuent à renforcer les droits de l'homme, notamment grâce à l'engagement de la Hongrie auprès de la CPI et à un chatbot basé sur l'IA qui vient en aide aux migrants en Italie.
news_summary_es: Los avances en materia de justicia y tecnología están contribuyendo a la defensa de los derechos humanos gracias a la colaboración de Hungría con la CPI y a un chatbot basado en inteligencia artificial que ayuda a los migrantes en Italia.

articles.title: Hungary’s Road Back to the Rule of Law Runs through the ICC
articles.summary:
Click to expand Image
          



  
        
  

        
                      
      
        
              Prime Minister-elect Peter Magyar, the Tisza Party's leader, speaks to the media after the preparatory meeting for the inaugural session of the Parliament in Budapest, Hungary, April 17, 2026.
                    © 2026 Robert Hegedus/MTI via AP Photo 
          
    


  
At his first international press conference, Hungary’s prime minister-elect Péter Magyar pledged to reverse his country’s move to leave the International Criminal Court (ICC).Magyar won a landslide victory in Hungary’s April 12 election, where he campaigned, among other things, on restoring rule of law and ties with the EU, opposing former Prime Minister Viktor Orbán.Today, ICC membership is required of EU candidate countries in order to accede to the EU which has made legally binding commitments to promote and protect the court. Magyar acknowledged that it is in Hungary’s interest to remain an ICC member, echoing earlier calls by Hungarian lawyers that the withdrawal would tarnish Hungary’s global standing.Orbán had announced Hungary’s intention to withdraw from the ICC in April 2025, while hosting Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu, who is wanted by the ICC for war crimes and crimes against humanity in Gaza. The withdrawal is set to take effect on 2 June 2026. If not halted, Hungary will become the only EU member state outside the ICC.The Magyar government has a short window to reverse course, and time is of the essence. With a supermajority in the parliament and clear precedents from other countries halting their own withdrawals from the court, nothing stands in the way of swift, decisive action.Notably, Magyar also publicly acknowledged Hungary’s obligations as an ICC member and committed to executing the court’s arrest warrant against Netanyahu should he visit Hungary for the 1956 Revolution Anniversary in October 2026. This is an important shift from the previous government, which flouted its legal obligation to arrest Netanyahu.It’s a powerful reminder to all individuals wanted by the court that their world has gotten smaller. Other EU leaders who have, at times, waivered in their own commitment to cooperate with the court should publicly project the same clarity.Once in office, Hungary’s new government should turn the page and restore respect for fundamental rights and democratic safeguards in the country. Reversing the Orbán government’s withdrawal from the ICC is one immediate step the new government should take to break with his predecessor’s anti-rights record and Hungary’s isolation within the EU.Magyar’s statements are a good start. He should now follow through and bring Hungary back into the community of states committed to ensuring justice for serious international crimes.
articles.content:
Click to expand Image Prime Minister-elect Peter Magyar, the Tisza Party's leader, speaks to the media after the preparatory meeting for the inaugural session of the Parliament in Budapest, Hungary, April 17, 2026. © 2026 Robert Hegedus/MTI via AP Photo

At his first international press conference, Hungary’s prime minister-elect Péter Magyar pledged to reverse his country’s move to leave the International Criminal Court (ICC).

Magyar won a landslide victory in Hungary’s April 12 election, where he campaigned, among other things, on restoring rule of law and ties with the EU, opposing former Prime Minister Viktor Orbán.

Today, ICC membership is required of EU candidate countries in order to accede to the EU which has made legally binding commitments to promote and protect the court. Magyar acknowledged that it is in Hungary’s interest to remain an ICC member, echoing earlier calls by Hungarian lawyers that the withdrawal would tarnish Hungary’s global standing.

Orbán had announced Hungary’s intention to withdraw from the ICC in April 2025, while hosting Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu, who is wanted by the ICC for war crimes and crimes against humanity in Gaza. The withdrawal is set to take effect on 2 June 2026. If not halted, Hungary will become the only EU member state outside the ICC.

The Magyar government has a short window to reverse course, and time is of the essence. With a supermajority in the parliament and clear precedents from other countries halting their own withdrawals from the court, nothing stands in the way of swift, decisive action.

Notably, Magyar also publicly acknowledged Hungary’s obligations as an ICC member and committed to executing the court’s arrest warrant against Netanyahu should he visit Hungary for the 1956 Revolution Anniversary in October 2026. This is an important shift from the previous government, which flouted its legal obligation to arrest Netanyahu.

It’s a powerful reminder to all individuals wanted by the court that their world has gotten smaller. Other EU leaders who have, at times, waivered in their own commitment to cooperate with the court should publicly project the same clarity.

Once in office, Hungary’s new government should turn the page and restore respect for fundamental rights and democratic safeguards in the country. Reversing the Orbán government’s withdrawal from the ICC is one immediate step the new government should take to break with his predecessor’s anti-rights record and Hungary’s isolation within the EU.

Magyar’s statements are a good start. He should now follow through and bring Hungary back into the community of states committed to ensuring justice for serious international crimes.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant political shift in Hungary toward restoring rule of law and recommitting to the International Criminal Court, which has broad implications for justice, human rights, and Hungary's reintegration with the EU. The new government's pledge to reverse the withdrawal from the ICC and enforce international arrest warrants represents a meaningful positive change with substantial societal and legal impact.
articles.personality_title: Hungary’s new leader pledges to rejoin International Criminal Court and restore rule of law
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Hungary had planned to leave the International Criminal Court (ICC), a global court that tries serious crimes like war crimes. This decision was made under former Prime Minister Viktor Orbán, and Hungary was set to become the only European Union (EU) country outside the ICC.

**What happened** – Péter Magyar, Hungary’s prime minister-elect after winning the April 2026 election, promised at his first international press conference to stop Hungary’s exit from the ICC. He also said Hungary would follow the court’s rules, including arresting Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu if he visits Hungary, as the ICC has issued a warrant for him.

**Impact** – Magyar’s promise marks a clear change in Hungary’s approach to justice and international law. Staying in the ICC helps Hungary rebuild trust with the EU, which requires candidate countries to be ICC members. It also shows Hungary’s commitment to following international laws and respecting human rights, which had been questioned under the previous government.

**What's next step** – Magyar’s government has a short time before the withdrawal takes effect in June 2026 to officially reverse the decision. With strong support in parliament, Hungary can quickly rejoin the ICC and start cooperating fully with the court. This move could improve Hungary’s relations with other countries and strengthen democracy at home.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Hungary’s new government aims to restore its place in the International Criminal Court, signaling a renewed commitment to justice and stronger ties with the European Union.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le nouveau dirigeant hongrois promet de réintégrer la Cour pénale internationale et de restaurer l'État de droit
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La Hongrie avait prévu de quitter la Cour pénale internationale (CPI), une cour mondiale qui juge les crimes graves comme les crimes de guerre. Cette décision a été prise sous l'ancien Premier ministre Viktor Orbán, et la Hongrie allait devenir le seul pays de l'Union européenne (UE) hors de la CPI.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Péter Magyar, le Premier ministre élu de Hongrie après sa victoire aux élections d'avril 2026, a promis lors de sa première conférence de presse internationale d'empêcher la sortie de la Hongrie de la CPI. Il a également déclaré que la Hongrie respecterait les règles de la cour, y compris l'arrestation du Premier ministre israélien Benjamin Netanyahu s'il venait en Hongrie, car la CPI a émis un mandat contre lui.

**Impact** – La promesse de Magyar marque un changement clair dans l'approche de la Hongrie envers la justice et le droit international. Rester membre de la CPI aide la Hongrie à reconstruire la confiance avec l'UE, qui exige que les pays candidats soient membres de la CPI. Cela montre aussi l'engagement de la Hongrie à respecter les lois internationales et les droits humains, ce qui avait été remis en question sous le gouvernement précédent.

**Prochaine étape** – Le gouvernement de Magyar dispose de peu de temps avant que le retrait ne prenne effet en juin 2026 pour annuler officiellement la décision. Avec une forte majorité au parlement, la Hongrie peut rapidement réintégrer la CPI et commencer à coopérer pleinement avec la cour. Cette démarche pourrait améliorer les relations de la Hongrie avec d'autres pays et renforcer la démocratie intérieure.

**Phrase clé** – Le nouveau gouvernement hongrois vise à restaurer sa place à la Cour pénale internationale, signe d'un engagement renouvelé envers la justice et des liens renforcés avec l'Union européenne.
articles.personality_title_es: El nuevo líder de Hungría promete reincorporarse a la Corte Penal Internacional y restaurar el estado de derecho
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Hungría había planeado salir de la Corte Penal Internacional (CPI), un tribunal mundial que juzga crímenes graves como crímenes de guerra. Esta decisión fue tomada bajo el ex primer ministro Viktor Orbán, y Hungría iba a ser el único país de la Unión Europea (UE) fuera de la CPI.

**Qué pasó** – Péter Magyar, el primer ministro electo de Hungría tras ganar las elecciones de abril de 2026, prometió en su primera conferencia de prensa internacional detener la salida de Hungría de la CPI. También dijo que Hungría cumpliría las reglas del tribunal, incluyendo arrestar al primer ministro israelí Benjamin Netanyahu si visita Hungría, ya que la CPI emitió una orden de arresto contra él.

**Impacto** – La promesa de Magyar representa un cambio claro en la postura de Hungría hacia la justicia y el derecho internacional. Mantenerse en la CPI ayuda a Hungría a reconstruir la confianza con la UE, que exige que los países candidatos sean miembros de la CPI. También muestra el compromiso de Hungría con las leyes internacionales y los derechos humanos, que fueron cuestionados bajo el gobierno anterior.

**Próximo paso** – El gobierno de Magyar tiene poco tiempo antes de que la salida se haga efectiva en junio de 2026 para revertir oficialmente la decisión. Con un fuerte apoyo en el parlamento, Hungría puede reincorporarse rápidamente a la CPI y comenzar a cooperar plenamente con el tribunal. Este paso podría mejorar las relaciones de Hungría con otros países y fortalecer la democracia interna.

**Frase clave** – El nuevo gobierno de Hungría busca restaurar su lugar en la Corte Penal Internacional, señalando un compromiso renovado con la justicia y vínculos más fuertes con la Unión Europea.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a symbolic courtroom with balanced scales glowing gently at the center, surrounded by intertwined ribbons in Hungary’s red, white, and green colors flowing toward a shining European Union star, set against a calm dawn sky over Budapest’s Parliament silhouette, evoking justice restored and renewed international commitment.

articles.title: ‘Sportellino’ is an AI chatbot who helps migrants navigate the maze of Italian bureaucracy
articles.summary: "Sportellino," a multilingual chatbot designed for migrants, is currently leading the way at the national level by setting a good example. Recently, the Italian government also launched similar tools.
articles.content:
This post is part of Global Voices’ April 2026 Spotlight series, “Human perspectives on AI.” This series will offer insight into how AI is being used in global majority countries, how its use and implementation are affecting individual communities, what this AI experiment might mean for future generations, and more. You can support this coverage by donating here.

Language and bureaucracy are the two biggest challenges for migrants arriving in Italy. The most common experience for a migrant is to stand in a waiting line, looking for information, desperate to complete papers and fill out forms — all necessities for being recognized as an asylum seeker or obtaining a residence permit. The country’s main language is Italian, while many have a basic level of English and sometimes some French or Spanish. There are more people in need than operators at migrant reception centers, and from this, an idea arose: an AI chatbot that gives step-by-step instructions and information to migrants. Creators imagined this could help ease the load for operators, too, by creating direct lines for specific needs, so people are not just asking for help at the wrong offices.

That idea became reality in July 2025 with the official launch of Sportellino (literally “little help desk”), an innovative multilingual chatbot available via WhatsApp or Telegram, free of charge and working alongside migrant centers and operators.

Global Voices spoke with Rebecca di Matteo, Head of Operations at Sportellino, a nonprofit, about the project and the challenges they’ve faced in helping migrants.

Global Voices (GV): What is Sportellino, and how has your chatbot helped migrants in their daily lives?

Rebecca Di Matteo (RDM): Sportellino was created within the Master’s program in Migration and Development at Sapienza University of Rome, responding to a very specific need: to simplify access to information that, for many migrants, is not immediate in their daily lives. This includes bureaucratic procedures, documents, healthcare services, guidance on rights, and administrative procedures that are often complex, fragmented, and difficult to understand, even for those with a good command of Italian. In this sense, the chatbot helps reduce confusion, misinformation, and feelings of frustration by offering clear, verified, and multilingual answers, accessible directly through messaging tools already in use every day, such as WhatsApp and Telegram. The service is free, anonymous, and available 24/7, and is based on a knowledge base built from official sources and information validated by industry professionals. To date, Sportellino is particularly effective in assisting users with practical issues related to daily life and access to rights, such as residence permits, civil registry registration, health insurance cards, choosing a GP [general practicioner], SPID (Public Digital Identity System), employment, school, and other public services. At the same time, the project is constantly evolving, and we are working to further improve our ability to respond to broader and more complex questions.

Language and access

The chatbot is currently available in English, French, Spanish, Arabic, Farsi, Pashto, Ukrainian, and other languages. According to Roma Capitale Magazine, the numbers speak for themselves: since the chatbot’s launch in July 2025, it has reached approximately 10,000 users (as of March 2026), with a peak of 2,189 new users in October 2025 alone.

Accessibility is easy: just scan the QR code, and a chat opens directly on WhatsApp or Telegram. It doesn’t ask for registration or personal data — use is completely anonymous, in line with the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) — and it’s ad-free. Sportellino does not collect any other personal information and never asks for sensitive data. They consciously chose to base this chatbot on an LLM developed by Mistral, a European company that adheres to some of the highest privacy standards in the industry. Furthermore, their server is located in Frankfurt, within the European Union, and all data is processed in full compliance with the GDPR.

GV: As Global Voices usually reports on people whose voices and experiences are rarely seen in mainstream media, can you share some firsthand examples?

RDM: A concrete and particularly representative case: a Nigerian man used the Sportellino chatbot to better understand the situation of labor exploitation he was experiencing, orient himself regarding his rights, and understand what forms of protection were available to him. Subsequently, thanks to the information received, he contacted us, the Sportellino operators, directly by email. From there, we were able to guide him to a local protection network capable of offering him concrete support.

The people behind the project recalled the direct impact seen by a group of migrants immediately:

When we presented the prototype in a house shared by asylum seekers in the Esquilino neighborhood of Rome, seeing people's enthusiasm and surprise as they tested the trial version for the first time was truly moving. In particular, many were impressed by the fact that Sportellino could respond in Arabic, explaining in simple terms how to prepare for important appointments and where to find free support in their area. It was a moment that made us understand, in a very concrete way, the potential of such a tool.

Their team is made up of people from Italy, Morocco, Ukraine, Germany, Argentina, and Senegal. They have built a knowledge base using official regulations, enriching it with practical information gathered from cultural mediators, social workers, and professionals specialized in asylum seekers. The app also informs users about average wait times, required documents, and useful addresses, helping users better prepare for appointments. In this case, AI complements, not replaces, human support. This chatbot can be a powerful force for promoting rights, inclusion, and respect.

GV: Talking about Italy, have you ever been contacted by the government to implement Sportellino in official projects?

RDM: We are not currently in direct contact with the government, but we are initiating collaborations with various local organizations, including Italian municipalities, departments, Immigration Help Desks, Anti-Violence Centers (CAVs), startups, associations, local institutions, Sapienza University of Rome, and third-sector organizations committed to facilitating access to rights and services every day.

GV: So, for the foreseeable future, will your AI chatbot replace humans or not?

RDM: We envision a model in which Sportellino can cover a significant portion of FAQs from foreigners, such as the most recurring and informative ones, while another portion continues to be addressed by physical help desks and operators, who perform invaluable work in the community. In this sense, Sportellino does not replace physical help desks but can become a complementary tool: it helps lighten the burden of simpler and more repetitive requests, allowing operators to devote more time, attention, and care to more complex cases, which require personalized, in-depth human intervention.

Another AI chatbot has been launched by the Italian government

The Italian Ministry of Labor and Social Policies launched its own chatbot designed for the Migrant Integration Portal on March 5, 2026. The PIM chatbot is a virtual assistant based on generative AI that speaks six languages (Italian, French, English, Spanish, Arabic, and Chinese).

GV: What do you think about the Ministry of Labor introducing a similar chatbot?
articles.reasoning: The article describes the launch and impact of 'Sportellino,' an AI chatbot that significantly improves access to bureaucratic and legal information for migrants in Italy. This tool addresses a broad societal challenge by helping thousands of migrants navigate complex systems, promoting inclusion, rights awareness, and reducing barriers caused by language and administrative complexity. It is a concrete, impactful innovation with measurable benefits for a vulnerable population, supported by detailed context and examples.
articles.personality_title: Sportellino chatbot helps migrants navigate Italy’s complex bureaucracy
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Migrants arriving in Italy often face two big problems: language barriers and complicated government paperwork. Many struggle to find clear information about important things like residence permits or healthcare. There are not enough staff at migrant centers to help everyone, which causes long waits and confusion.

**What happened** – In July 2025, a nonprofit group launched Sportellino, a free chatbot that migrants can use on WhatsApp or Telegram. It answers questions in many languages like English, Arabic, Spanish, and Ukrainian. The chatbot gives clear, step-by-step help about papers, healthcare, jobs, and other services. It uses official information and respects user privacy. By March 2026, around 10,000 people had used it, including a Nigerian man who learned how to protect himself from labor exploitation.

**Impact** – Sportellino makes it easier for migrants to understand and complete important procedures without waiting in long lines or getting lost in confusing offices. It offers help anytime and in users’ own languages, reducing frustration and mistakes. The chatbot supports, but does not replace, human workers by handling common questions and freeing staff to focus on more complex cases.

**What's next step** – The team plans to improve Sportellino to answer more detailed questions and is working with local organizations and universities to expand its reach. Meanwhile, the Italian government launched its own AI chatbot in March 2026 to assist migrants on an official platform, showing growing interest in using AI for migrant support.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Sportellino is a free, multilingual AI chatbot that helps migrants in Italy understand and complete bureaucratic procedures, easing their daily challenges and supporting human services.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le chatbot Sportellino aide les migrants à naviguer dans la bureaucratie italienne
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les migrants arrivant en Italie rencontrent souvent deux grands obstacles : la barrière de la langue et les démarches administratives compliquées. Beaucoup ont du mal à trouver des informations claires sur des sujets importants comme les permis de séjour ou les soins de santé. Les centres d’accueil manquent de personnel pour aider tout le monde, ce qui cause de longues attentes et de la confusion.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – En juillet 2025, un groupe à but non lucratif a lancé Sportellino, un chatbot gratuit accessible via WhatsApp ou Telegram. Il répond aux questions en plusieurs langues comme l’anglais, l’arabe, l’espagnol et l’ukrainien. Le chatbot donne des instructions claires et étape par étape sur les papiers, les soins, le travail et d’autres services. Il utilise des informations officielles et respecte la vie privée des utilisateurs. En mars 2026, environ 10 000 personnes l’avaient utilisé, dont un homme nigérian qui a appris à se protéger contre l’exploitation au travail.

**Impact** – Sportellino facilite la compréhension et la réalisation des démarches importantes sans longues files d’attente ni perte dans les bureaux complexes. Il offre une aide à toute heure et dans la langue de l’utilisateur, réduisant la frustration et les erreurs. Le chatbot soutient, sans remplacer, les travailleurs humains en traitant les questions courantes et en libérant du temps pour les cas plus complexes.

**Prochaine étape** – L’équipe prévoit d’améliorer Sportellino pour répondre à des questions plus détaillées et collabore avec des organisations locales et des universités pour étendre son usage. Par ailleurs, le gouvernement italien a lancé en mars 2026 son propre chatbot d’IA pour aider les migrants sur une plateforme officielle, montrant un intérêt croissant pour l’IA dans ce domaine.

**En une phrase** – Sportellino est un chatbot IA gratuit et multilingue qui aide les migrants en Italie à comprendre et accomplir leurs démarches administratives, facilitant leur quotidien et soutenant les services humains.
articles.personality_title_es: El chatbot Sportellino ayuda a los migrantes a navegar la burocracia italiana
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los migrantes que llegan a Italia enfrentan dos grandes problemas: barreras del idioma y trámites gubernamentales complicados. Muchos tienen dificultades para encontrar información clara sobre temas importantes como permisos de residencia o atención médica. No hay suficientes empleados en los centros de migrantes para ayudar a todos, lo que genera largas esperas y confusión.

**Qué pasó** – En julio de 2025, un grupo sin fines de lucro lanzó Sportellino, un chatbot gratuito que los migrantes pueden usar en WhatsApp o Telegram. Responde preguntas en varios idiomas como inglés, árabe, español y ucraniano. El chatbot ofrece ayuda clara y paso a paso sobre documentos, salud, trabajo y otros servicios. Usa información oficial y protege la privacidad de los usuarios. Para marzo de 2026, unas 10,000 personas lo habían usado, incluyendo a un hombre nigeriano que aprendió a protegerse de la explotación laboral.

**Impacto** – Sportellino facilita que los migrantes comprendan y completen trámites importantes sin largas filas ni confusión. Ofrece ayuda a cualquier hora y en el idioma del usuario, reduciendo frustración y errores. El chatbot apoya, pero no reemplaza, a los trabajadores humanos al atender preguntas comunes y permitir que el personal se enfoque en casos más complejos.

**Próximo paso** – El equipo planea mejorar Sportellino para responder preguntas más detalladas y trabaja con organizaciones locales y universidades para ampliar su uso. Mientras tanto, el gobierno italiano lanzó en marzo de 2026 su propio chatbot de IA para ayudar a migrantes en una plataforma oficial, mostrando un interés creciente en usar IA para este fin.

**Resumen en una frase** – Sportellino es un chatbot de IA gratuito y multilingüe que ayuda a los migrantes en Italia a entender y realizar trámites burocráticos, facilitando su vida diaria y apoyando los servicios humanos.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a glowing, open maze made of softly colored parchment-like walls, with diverse, stylized paper boats carrying folded multilingual letters and simple icons of chat bubbles navigating its pathways, gently guided by a radiant, abstract lantern symbolizing an AI chatbot’s light, set against a calm, neutral background.
